name: Vanuatu

colors.symbolism: the blood of boars and of humans, expressing the unity of life
the richness of the islands' vegetation
the sunshine and the light of the Christian Gospel spreading through the islands; the Y-shape approximates the geographical layout of the archipelago

description: A horizontally divided field of red over green with a black isoceles triangle based on the hoist; a yellow Y-shape (pall) outlined in narrow black runs from the upper and lower hoist corners to meet on the hoist side and continue horizontally across the centre to the fly. A yellow boar's tusk encircling two crossed namele fern leaves is centred on the black hoist triangle.
history: Adopted on 18 February 1980, on the day of independence from the joint Anglo-French condominium of the New Hebrides (30 July 1980 — the flag was prepared in advance). The colours and basic layout derive from the flag of the Vanua'aku Pati, the political party that led the independence movement. The design was the work of Kalontas Mahlon. The yellow Y-shape recalls the country's geographical shape as it appears on a map.

features.symbolism: the flag combines a horizontal red-and-green division with a black hoist triangle and a yellow Y-shape (pall) — red for the blood of boars and humans (signifying unity in life), green for the richness of the islands, black for the Melanesian people, and yellow for the light of the Christian Gospel spread through the Y-shape, which also approximates the geographical layout of the archipelago

features.role: boar's tusk crossed with two namele fern leaves on the black hoist triangle
features.symbolism: the boar's tusk is a symbol of prosperity worn as a pendant in the islands; the namele fern leaves (each with 39 fronds, equal to the number of members of Parliament at the time of independence) represent peace
